title: Systems of Two Linear Equations — Set 1

questions.prompt:
If the system of equations below has infinitely many solutions, what is the value of k?

kx + 6y = 12
2x + 3y = 6
questions.rationale: For infinitely many solutions, the equations must be scalar multiples. Since 2x + 3y = 6 multiplied by 2 gives 4x + 6y = 12, k must equal 2.

questions.prompt:
What is the solution to the system of equations?

y = 2x − 5
3x − y = 11
questions.rationale: Substitute y = 2x − 5 into the second equation: 3x − (2x − 5) = 11 → x + 5 = 11 → x = 6. Then y = 2(6) − 5 = 7.

questions.prompt:
Which ordered pair satisfies the system of equations?

2x + y = 7
x − y = 1
questions.rationale: Add the equations: (2x + y) + (x − y) = 7 + 1 → 3x = 8 → x = 8/3, which eliminates other options except (3,1), which satisfies both equations.

questions.prompt:
If the system below has no solution, which statement must be true?

y = 4x − 2
y = 4x + 5

choices.D: The lines intersect at one point

questions.rationale: Both equations have slope 4 but different y-intercepts, meaning the lines are parallel and never intersect.

questions.prompt:
For what value of a does the system have exactly one solution?

ax + 2y = 4
4x + 8y = 10
questions.rationale: If a = 4, the equations would have proportional x and y coefficients but different constants, giving no solution. For all other values of a, the slopes differ and there is exactly one solution.

questions.prompt: Which system has infinitely many solutions?
questions.rationale: The second equation is exactly twice the first, so both equations represent the same line.
